title: Massive problem budget failed to fix
full_text:
Australia’s gross debt will surpass $1 trillion in the upcoming months despite the federal government unveiling the most ambitious budget reforms in years.
Touted as the biggest changes made in 25 years, Treasurer Jim Chalmers announced sweeping reform, including the curbing of negative gearing and the removal of the per cent 50 capital gains tax (CGT) discount.
S&P Global analysts Martin Foo and Anthony Walker said the ambitious changes only marginally improved the budget bottom line.
“Nevertheless, central government gross debt will cross the $1 trillion mark within the next few months, sharpening political focus on the national balance sheet,” they said in a joint statement.
“Net general government debt, by our measures, has risen 13 percentage points of GDP since 2019.”
The national debt is also expected to rise over the next four years.
According to the Treasury department, gross national debt will jump from $928.6bn in 2024-2025 to $982bn by the end of the 2026 financial year.
This will continue to rise over the four-year forward estimates, with Australia cracking the $1 trillion debt mark in 2027.
In 2027, debt is tipped to rise to $1.051 trillion before lifting by $70bn to $1.12 trillion in 2028.
By the end of the forward estimates, Australia is projected to owe $1.249 trillion as gross debt peaks at 35.6 per cent of GDP.
This is tipped to be the peak of the nation’s debt, as Australia starts paying it back over the next decade.
By 2037, Australia’s gross debt is expected to fall back to 27.2 per cent of GDP.
Despite Australia’s growing debt, Mr Walker and Mr Foo acknowledge it is still low relative to global standards.
The pair also said Australia’s rising commodity prices would temporarily boost revenue, which would help slow down the growing debt.
“The government has revised tax receipts upward by $34.5bn (1.2 per cent of GDP) over five years on the back of higher commodity prices, which translate into corporate profits, and growth in income tax and superannuation fund collections,” they said.
“But this windfall revenue, coupled with savings measures, will be partly channelled into new spending to position Australia for a more uncertain world.”
Changes to take pressure off workers
Leading the tax reforms were changes to negative gearing and the CGT discount.
Under the CGT changes, the 50 per cent discount has been scrapped and returned to indexation, while negative gearing will now be restricted to new builds.
Investors will be given a one-year grace period before the changes take affect for assets bought after July 1, 2027.
Investors will be expected to have their assets valued independently or by the tax man by that date for tax purposes.
“Since 1999, house prices have risen over 400 per cent, more than twice as fast as average incomes,” Mr Chalmers said.
“Our tax changes will help about 75,000 Australians achieve the dream of home ownership.”
The measures are expected to raise $3.6bn in the 2027-2028 financial year.
Actuaries Institute chief executive Elayne Grace said the budget took a step in the right direction, with the economic and tax reforms announced on Tuesday night helping to make Australia fairer for the next generation.
“Australia is currently not meeting the long-held hope that each generation will leave the next a little better off – particularly around housing, climate and the environment,” she said.
“Many young people rely on the ‘Bank of Mum and Dad’ to buy a home, which advantages people born into wealthier families.
“The consequences of lower home ownership rates extend into retirement. Homeowners over 65 enjoy disposable incomes 2.5 times higher than non-homeowners – a stark divide in financial security.”
National Shelter, a peak body that aims to improve housing outcomes for low-income Aussies, called the budget a “turning point for housing” but urged the government to go a step further to help increase home ownership.
“It respects existing investment decisions, avoids unnecessary market disruption, and starts to unwind tax settings that have long skewed housing outcomes, disproportionably impacting low-moderate households,” National Shelter chief executive Jackson Hill said.
“This budget begins to rebalance the system back towards first-home buyers, renters and the delivery of new homes that the country desperately needs.”
But Mr Hill warns more still needs to be done.
He said Australia faced a structural housing shortfall that tax reform alone wouldn’t resolve, as the country was projected to be short about 640,000 social and affordable homes by 2041.
The Real Estate Institute of Australia said the changes to negative gearing and the CGT discount would instead undermine housing supply.
REIA resident Jacob Caine said modelling by Qaive and Tulipwood indicated the reforms may result in about 25,500 fewer homes being built over the next five years.
“Australia is already well behind the National Housing Accord target of 1.2 million new homes, so we need policies that support investment and new housing supply, not policies that make delivery harder,” Mr Caine said.
“Private investment plays a critical role in Australia’s housing system.
“At a time of acute rental stress and chronic undersupply, policy settings should be encouraging more investment into housing, not creating uncertainty or reducing confidence.”
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
Document NLNEWW0020260513em5d0015s
summary: Australia's gross debt is set to exceed $1 trillion despite significant budget reforms aimed at curbing negative gearing and capital gains tax, with projected debt rising to $1.249 trillion by 2028.
author: Cameron Micallef
heading: Actuaries
key_points: Treasurer Jim Chalmers announced major tax reforms including the removal of the 50% capital gains tax discount and restrictions on negative gearing.
Gross national debt is expected to rise from $928.6 billion in 2024-2025 to $1.051 trillion by 2027.
The budget reforms aim to improve housing affordability but face criticism for potentially reducing housing supply.
